name: delivery
description: portitor's release pipeline: how the `portitor` binary gets from a tagged commit to a downloadable, verifiable artifact. Binaries only — the container image is a local build artifact (`docker build -t portitor .`), never published. Generic module: the executable `.goreleaser.yaml` and the CI workflow files are the source of truth for how artifacts are built; this module documents intent and does not define a bespoke delivery-metadata schema. NOTE: this module.json is a hand-authored SEED in spexmachina's format — identity hashes (requirement id/preq_id, component spec_node_id) and spec/.snapshot.json are generated when run through `spex` / the /spec skill; they are intentionally omitted here.

requirements.title: Cross-architecture binary release
requirements.description: Release builds target linux and darwin, each amd64 and arm64 (four targets, no windows — portitor is a git-server-side gate, never installed on an agent's machine). `.goreleaser.yaml`'s build matrix (goos × goarch) is the sole definition of the target set.

requirements.title: Tiered CI by trigger
requirements.description: pull_request runs the fast gate (go build, go vet, go test). push to main runs the same gate plus `-race`. A nightly schedule fuzzes every `func Fuzz*` discovered in the tree (spec/reviews/2026-07-18.md §5's plan), each under a bounded -fuzztime, with no hardcoded target list to fall out of sync as targets are added. push of a `v*` tag runs the release pipeline. Each workflow sets `concurrency: {cancel-in-progress: true}` scoped to its own ref, and the CI workflow ignores docs-only changes (`**.md`, `spec/**`) so prose edits don't burn a runner.

requirements.title: Signed release artifacts
requirements.description: Every release archive is signed with SSHSIG (`ssh-keygen -Y sign -n file`, Ed25519) via GoReleaser's `signs` block, producing one `.sig` per archive. The secret key is supplied to the release workflow as the `SSH_SIGNING_KEY` repository secret, written to a runner-local temp file (chmod 600) for the duration of the job and removed afterward — the key material never appears in an argv or a template-expanded command line. The corresponding public key is published in README.md so a downloader can verify with `ssh-keygen -Y verify` against an `allowed_signers` line offline, independent of GitHub's availability.

requirements.title: SLSA build provenance
requirements.description: `actions/attest-build-provenance` runs in the release workflow (permissions `id-token: write`, `attestations: write`) over every published archive, producing an attestation verifiable with `gh attestation verify` — a second, GitHub-native chain of custody independent of the SSHSIG signature.

requirements.title: Machine-readable release manifest
requirements.description: `scripts/generate-manifest.sh` derives `manifest.json` from GoReleaser's own `dist/artifacts.json` (per-archive name/goos/goarch/format) and `dist/metadata.json` (version/commit/date) — recomputing each artifact's sha256 and size directly from the archive bytes on disk rather than trusting an internal field name, so the manifest is self-verifying against what actually shipped. Shape: `{schema_version, tool, version, git_sha, git_ref, built_at, status, artifacts:[{name, target, sha256, size_bytes, archive_format}]}`. It is attached to the GitHub release alongside the binaries so a downstream consumer (e.g. a Nix overlay) can pin a per-target checksum programmatically instead of hand-copying it out of a release page. The shape is generic — no portitor-specific field — and it is a derived, disposable projection: `.goreleaser.yaml` remains the one source of truth for how artifacts are actually built.

requirements.title: Binaries-only release channel
requirements.description: The GitHub Releases page is the only publication channel: each release carries the four archives, a consolidated `checksums.txt`, one `.sha256` per archive, one SSHSIG `.sig` per archive, `manifest.json`, and the `install.sh` installer alongside its SSHSIG `install.sh.sig` signature (published and signed with the same release key as the archives). No container image or package-manager formula is published by this pipeline — the Dockerfile is a local build artifact the operator runs `docker build` against themselves.

requirements.title: In-place self-update via the embedded installer
requirements.description: `portitor upgrade` updates the installed standalone binary in place by reusing the signed install.sh — embedded into the binary and run in an upgrade mode against the running binary's own path — never a Go reimplementation of the update. install.sh gains an opt-in upgrade mode (--upgrade --target <path>) that keeps its resolve/download/ssh-keygen-Y-verify logic untouched and adds: a safe self-replace (move-aside + rename(2), never install/cp over the running file, to avoid ETXTBSY); a forward-only version guard (equal → already-up-to-date, exit 0; on the latest path — VERSION unset — a resolved latest OLDER than current is a rollback anomaly, hard-refused and NOT overridable, there being no --force; on the explicit path — VERSION set, i.e. --version vX.Y.Z — the named release installs in any direction with an 'as explicitly requested' notice; a signature is authenticity not freshness, so a latest that moved backward is treated as a compromised origin); --rollback (restore <target>.bak, and auto-restore it on any mid-replace failure); --check/--dry-run (report availability only, exit 0 even when latest is older than installed, flagging it as an anomaly WARNING); and RequireRoot with NO auto-sudo (probe target-dir writability and tell the operator to elevate, rather than self-invoking sudo as first-install mode may). first-install mode (no flags) is behaviour-preserving.

requirements.title: Embedded installer is byte-identical to the released one
requirements.description: The canonical install.sh lives at the repo root (uploaded by release.yml, documented in the README). //go:embed cannot traverse '..', so a byte-identical copy is kept at cmd/portitor/install.sh, refreshed by `//go:generate cp ../../install.sh install.sh`, and a Go test (TestUpgradeEmbeddedMatchesReleased) fails the build if the two diverge. This identity is the security argument for self-update: because the embedded script is provably the same audited, signed installer a user runs by hand, `upgrade` needs no separate fetch-and-verify-the-script step — there is nothing to substitute. install.sh also reads test-only PORTITOR_API_BASE/PORTITOR_DL_BASE origin overrides (defaulting to production) so a fake-origin harness can drive the real script; these are not trust-sensitive (verification still gates whatever the origin serves) and the trust anchor SIGNING_PUBKEY is never env-overridable.

requirements.title: Deterministic, ldflags-stamped builds
requirements.description: Builds run with `CGO_ENABLED=0`, `-trimpath`, and linker flags `-s -w` plus `-X main.version=… -X main.commit=… -X main.date=…` sourced from the triggering tag, commit, and build time — so `portitor version` on a downloaded binary reports exactly what it shipped as, with no reliance on runtime environment to reconstruct provenance.

requirements.title: Locally provable before automation runs
requirements.description: The identical `.goreleaser.yaml` and `scripts/generate-manifest.sh` that the release workflow drives also run unmodified via `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean` on a developer machine (snapshot mode skips publish/tag requirements) — the full build → archive → sign → checksum → manifest chain is provable without pushing a tag, holding CI secrets, or touching GitHub Actions.

components.name: CIPipeline
components.description: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` + `fuzz.yml`: the pull_request/push-to-main fast-then-race gate (go build, go vet, go test, and -race on push only), and the nightly fuzz job that discovers every `func Fuzz*` in the tree at run time via a two-line grep/awk loop (no hardcoded target list) and runs each with a bounded -fuzztime, uploading any failing corpus entry as a workflow artifact on failure.

components.name: ReleasePipeline
components.description: `.goreleaser.yaml` + `.github/workflows/release.yml`: on push of a `v*` tag, cross-compiles linux/darwin × amd64/arm64 via GoReleaser, archives each binary with README.md/LICENSE, computes per-artifact + consolidated sha256 checksums, and publishes the GitHub release — then layers on the per-artifact `.sha256` files, `manifest.json`, and the SLSA provenance attestation that GoReleaser itself does not produce.

components.name: Manifest
components.description: `scripts/generate-manifest.sh`: turns GoReleaser's `dist/artifacts.json` + `dist/metadata.json` into `manifest.json`, self-verifying each artifact's sha256/size from the archive bytes on disk. Generic — the shape is repo-agnostic and portitor defines no bespoke delivery-metadata schema beyond it; `.goreleaser.yaml` is the source of truth this script reads from, never the other way around.

components.name: Signing
components.description: GoReleaser's `signs` block invokes `ssh-keygen -Y sign -n file` (SSHSIG) against each archive with a key the release workflow materializes from the `SSH_SIGNING_KEY` secret into a runner-local temp file (chmod 600; never argv, never a template-expanded command line) and removes afterward, producing one `.sig` per archive. The public key is published in README.md and a verifier checks it with `ssh-keygen -Y verify` against an `allowed_signers` line — the same key and namespace (`-n file`) install.sh embeds, so first-install, maximal, and self-update all trust one anchor. Signing, the consolidated/per-artifact sha256 checksums, and the SLSA attestation are three independent ways to trust a downloaded binary — none is a substitute for another.

components.name: SelfUpdate
components.description: install.sh's upgrade mode (opt-in via --upgrade --target <path>), embedded into the binary and driven by `portitor upgrade`: shared resolve/download/SSHSIG-verify with first-install, plus safe self-replace (move-aside + rename to avoid ETXTBSY), a forward-only version guard (equal → no-op; a resolved latest older than installed is a non-overridable rollback-anomaly refusal on the latest path, while an explicitly named --version installs any direction; there is no --force), --rollback, --check (report-only, warns-not-refuses on an older latest), and RequireRoot-no-sudo. Includes the embedded==released byte-identity invariant (cmd/portitor/install.sh via go:generate, enforced by a go test) and the test-only origin hooks. The Go front-end is the cli module's UpgradeCommand.
